metadata.title: Global Maritime Chokepoints and Shipping Lanes
metadata.description: Critical maritime chokepoints and major shipping routes for geopolitical analysis

properties.name: Strait of Hormuz
properties.strategic_note: Handles roughly 21% of global petroleum liquids consumption. Closure would eliminate the only sea route out of the Persian Gulf, with no pipeline alternatives at comparable capacity.

properties.name: Strait of Malacca
properties.strategic_note: Carries approximately 25-30% of global maritime trade, including 80% of China's oil imports. The narrowest point at Phillips Channel is only 2.8 km wide, creating a critical bottleneck for East Asian energy security.

properties.name: Suez Canal
properties.strategic_note: Handles roughly 12-15% of global trade and 7-10% of global oil. Eliminates the 6,000 nm detour around Africa. The 2021 Ever Given blockage and 2024 Houthi disruptions demonstrated systemic fragility.

properties.name: Bab el-Mandeb
properties.strategic_note: Controls southern access to the Red Sea and thus the Suez Canal. Houthi attacks since late 2023 forced major rerouting of commercial traffic around the Cape of Good Hope, demonstrating how non-state actors can weaponize chokepoint proximity.

properties.name: Taiwan Strait
properties.daily_traffic: ~88% of world's largest container ships transit annually
properties.strategic_note: Beyond shipping volume, the strait's significance is defined by Taiwan's dominance of advanced semiconductor fabrication (TSMC produces ~90% of sub-7nm chips). A blockade would be simultaneously a maritime, trade, and technology crisis.

properties.name: Panama Canal
properties.strategic_note: Connects Atlantic and Pacific, handling ~5% of global maritime trade. Increasingly vulnerable to drought-induced water level drops that restrict vessel drafts. 2023-24 drought forced significant transit reductions, exposing climate vulnerability.

properties.name: Turkish Straits (Bosphorus/Dardanelles)
properties.strategic_note: Only maritime access from the Black Sea to the Mediterranean. Governed by the 1936 Montreux Convention, which gives Turkey control over warship transit. Critical for Russian naval power projection and grain/energy exports from the Black Sea region.

properties.name: Danish Straits (Oresund/Great Belt/Little Belt)
properties.strategic_note: Only maritime access to the Baltic Sea. Critical for Russian energy exports and Nordic/Baltic state trade. NATO's Baltic strategy depends on controlling these passages. Infrastructure sabotage (Nord Stream pipelines, Balticconnector) has heightened awareness.

properties.name: Strait of Gibraltar
properties.strategic_note: Only natural connection between the Atlantic and Mediterranean. NATO controls both shores. Critical for Mediterranean trade access and US/European naval operations.

properties.name: Cape of Good Hope
properties.daily_traffic: variable, surges during Suez/Bab el-Mandeb disruptions
properties.strategic_note: Not a narrow strait but a strategic waypoint. Primary alternative when Suez or Bab el-Mandeb is disrupted, adding 10-14 days to Europe-Asia routes. Traffic surged significantly after 2024 Houthi attacks forced rerouting.

properties.name: Lombok Strait
properties.strategic_note: Primary alternative to the Strait of Malacca for deep-draft vessels (VLCCs and ULCCs). Deeper and wider than Malacca, making it the preferred route for supertankers carrying Middle Eastern oil to East Asia.

properties.name: Korea Strait / Tsushima Strait
properties.strategic_note: Controls access between the Sea of Japan and the East China Sea. Critical for Russian Pacific Fleet movements from Vladivostok. Japan-Korea strategic competition adds political complexity to what is an essential trade corridor for Northeast Asia.

properties.name: Mozambique Channel
properties.strategic_note: Growing importance due to East African LNG development (Mozambique) and as a transit route for Cape of Good Hope diversions. China has expanded naval presence in the region. Insurgency in northern Mozambique threatens onshore LNG infrastructure.

properties.name: GIUK Gap
properties.strategic_note: Greenland-Iceland-UK gap controls Russian Northern Fleet access to the North Atlantic. Cold War-era SOSUS arrays monitored Soviet submarine transits. NATO's renewed focus on anti-submarine warfare has restored its strategic priority after post-Cold War neglect.

properties.name: Luzon Strait
properties.strategic_note: Primary deep-water passage between the South China Sea and the Pacific Ocean. Critical for submarine operations (deep Bashi Channel allows submerged transit). PLA Navy must control this strait to break out of the first island chain.

properties.name: Persian Gulf to East Asia Oil Route (via Malacca)
properties.strategic_note: The most strategically significant energy corridor on Earth. Carries the majority of Middle Eastern crude oil to China, Japan, and South Korea. Passes through three critical chokepoints: Hormuz, Malacca, and the South China Sea.

properties.name: Persian Gulf to Europe Oil Route (via Suez)
properties.strategic_note: Primary energy supply route for European oil imports from the Middle East. Passes through Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb, Suez, and Gibraltar. The entire route was disrupted by Houthi attacks in 2024, forcing Cape rerouting.

properties.name: East Asia to Europe Container Route (via Suez)
properties.strategic_note: The world's highest-value shipping lane, carrying manufactured goods from Chinese/East Asian factories to European consumers. Concentration of value in a single corridor creates systemic vulnerability to chokepoint disruption.

properties.name: East Asia to US West Coast Transpacific Route
properties.strategic_note: Largest bilateral trade lane by volume. Follows great circle route via North Pacific. Concentrated at receiving ports of Los Angeles/Long Beach. Supply chain disruptions here cascade through US retail and manufacturing.

properties.name: East Asia to US East Coast (via Panama/Suez)
properties.strategic_note: Two route options: via Panama Canal (shorter) or via Suez and transatlantic (for larger vessels). Panama Canal draft restrictions push mega-ships to the longer Suez route. Route choice vulnerability to both Panama drought and Red Sea disruption.

properties.name: Australia to China Bulk Route
properties.strategic_note: Carries iron ore (from Pilbara), coal, and LNG that are essential inputs to Chinese steel production and energy. Australia supplies ~60% of China's iron ore. Political tensions since 2020 have not disrupted iron ore trade due to lack of alternatives at scale.

properties.name: Brazil to China Bulk Route (iron ore/soybeans)
properties.strategic_note: Carries iron ore from Vale's operations and soybeans from Mato Grosso. Longest major bulk route (~11,000 nm). Brazil has displaced the US as China's primary soybean supplier, a structural shift accelerated by the US-China trade war.

properties.name: US Gulf to Europe LNG/Oil Route
properties.strategic_note: Transformed from minor route to critical energy corridor after 2022. US LNG exports to Europe surged following Russian gas cutoff, making this lane central to European energy security. Sabine Pass, Cameron, and Freeport LNG terminals are key origin points.

properties.name: West Africa to China Oil Route
properties.strategic_note: Nigerian and Angolan crude exports to China. Route typically goes via Cape of Good Hope. China has invested heavily in African oil production (CNOOC in Nigeria, Sinopec in Angola) to diversify away from Middle Eastern dependence.

properties.name: Northern Sea Route (Russia Arctic to Asia)
properties.strategic_note: Emerging route along Russia's Arctic coast, reducing Europe/Russia-to-Asia distances by 40% vs. Suez. Currently seasonal and icebreaker-dependent. Russia is investing heavily (Novatek Yamal/Arctic LNG-2). Climate change is extending navigable season. Potential to reshape Eurasian trade geography long-term.

properties.name: Middle East to India Oil Route
properties.strategic_note: Short but high-volume route supplying ~85% of India's crude oil imports. India has become the world's third-largest oil consumer. The route's brevity (2-3 days) provides India a structural advantage in energy security relative to East Asian competitors.

properties.name: Southeast Asia to East Asia Components/Manufacturing Route
properties.strategic_note: Carries intermediate manufactured goods, electronic components, and raw materials within the East Asian production network. Reflects the dense intra-regional supply chains that define contemporary Asian manufacturing. This is the physical infrastructure of chanyuan politics.
